notes: Curated by local experts. Priority 1-10 (10=must include).

restaurants.name: Dishoom
restaurants.description: Iconic Bombay-style cafe beloved by Londoners. Legendary bacon naan at breakfast.
restaurants.must_try: Bacon naan roll, black dal, house black chai
restaurants.booking_tip: Book weeks ahead or queue — the queue moves fast and is worth it

restaurants.name: St. John Restaurant
restaurants.description: Fergus Henderson's legendary restaurant that invented nose-to-tail eating. A London institution since 1994.
restaurants.must_try: Bone marrow and parsley salad, Welsh rarebit
restaurants.booking_tip: Book at least a week ahead. The bar does walk-ins.

restaurants.name: Bao Fitzrovia
restaurants.description: Tiny, minimalist Taiwanese bao spot with cult following. The classic pork bao is legendary.
restaurants.must_try: Classic bao, fried chicken bao, trotter nuggets
restaurants.booking_tip: No reservations — queue from opening or go at off-peak times (3-5pm)

restaurants.name: Maltby Street Market
restaurants.description: London's best-kept food market secret under the railway arches. Producers sell direct, no tourist markup.
restaurants.must_try: Anything from Monmouth Coffee, Kappacasein raclette, Neal's Yard cheese
restaurants.booking_tip: Saturday mornings 10am-5pm only. Cash and card both accepted.

restaurants.name: Lyles
restaurants.description: No-choice set menu lunch and dinner. James Lowe's cooking is some of London's most exciting. Michelin starred.
restaurants.must_try: Whatever is on the set menu — it changes daily
restaurants.booking_tip: Lunch is better value than dinner. Book 2-3 weeks ahead.

restaurants.name: E. Pellici
restaurants.description: Grade II listed 1946 caff run by the same family for 120 years. Full English next to Art Nouveau marquetry.
restaurants.must_try: Full English breakfast, bubble and squeak
restaurants.booking_tip: Cash only. Opens 7am, closes 4pm. Go for breakfast.

restaurants.name: Smoking Goat
restaurants.description: Buzzing Thai BBQ bar with natural wine and ferociously good food. A Shoreditch staple.
restaurants.must_try: Smoked fish sauce wings, crab fried rice
restaurants.booking_tip: Book ahead for dinner. Bar stools available walk-in.

restaurants.name: Borough Market
restaurants.description: London's most famous food market, trading since the 13th century. Go early, go hungry.
restaurants.must_try: Raclette from Kappacasein, scotch egg from Scotchtails, bread from Bread Ahead
restaurants.booking_tip: Wednesday-Saturday. Saturday is busiest. Arrive before 10am for elbow room.

excursions.name: Columbia Road Flower Market
excursions.description: Sunday morning flower market on a Victorian street. Vendors shout, locals buy armfuls of flowers. Magical.
excursions.booking_tip: Sundays only, 8am-3pm. Peak 9-11am. Go for the street atmosphere as much as the flowers.

excursions.name: Crossness Pumping Station
excursions.description: Jaw-dropping Victorian cathedral of sewage engineering. Ornate cast iron inside a massive pump house. One of London's greatest hidden buildings.
excursions.booking_tip: Open days only — check crossness.org.uk. Worth planning a trip around.

excursions.name: Parkland Walk
excursions.description: London's longest local nature reserve, following a disused railway line through north London. Spriggan sculpture mid-route.
excursions.booking_tip: Best April-October. Dog-friendly. Start at Finsbury Park end.

excursions.name: Dennis Severs' House
excursions.description: A preserved 18th-century silk weaver's house where you explore rooms as if the family just stepped out. Completely silent. Utterly immersive.
excursions.booking_tip: Book online at dennissevershouse.co.uk. Monday evenings are candlelit — unmissable.

excursions.name: Brixton Market & Brixton Village
excursions.description: Covered market arcade with Caribbean food stalls, independent restaurants, and record shops. The heartbeat of south London.
excursions.booking_tip: Wednesday-Sunday. Lunch is the best time. Pop Records on the corner is a must.

excursions.name: Thames Path walk: Tower Bridge to Greenwich
excursions.description: Walk the south bank of the Thames through Bermondsey, Rotherhithe, and Deptford to Greenwich. Passes through rarely-touristed working docklands.
excursions.booking_tip: Do it eastbound so the wind is mostly behind you. Grab lunch in Deptford Market Yard.

excursions.name: Highgate Cemetery West
excursions.description: Guided tour through one of the Magnificent Seven Victorian cemeteries. Egyptian Avenue and the Circle of Lebanon are extraordinary.
excursions.booking_tip: West side requires guided tour (worth it). Book at highgatecemetery.org.

hidden_gems.name: Leake Street Arches
hidden_gems.description: London's only legal graffiti tunnel under Waterloo station. Artists work here daily. Completely free and changes constantly.

hidden_gems.name: God's Own Junkyard
hidden_gems.description: A warehouse crammed floor-to-ceiling with neon signs from films, shows, and defunct businesses. Free entry, deeply surreal.

hidden_gems.name: Viktor Wynd Museum of Curiosities
hidden_gems.description: The most gloriously chaotic cabinet of curiosities in London. Two-headed animals, celebrity hair, Dodo bones, all in a tiny basement bar.

hidden_gems.name: Bevis Marks Synagogue
hidden_gems.description: Britain's oldest synagogue (1701), unchanged since it was built. The candle-lit interior is breathtaking and almost no tourists visit.

hidden_gems.name: The Walthamstow Wetlands
hidden_gems.description: Europe's largest urban wetland reserve — 10 Victorian reservoirs now a nature reserve 20 minutes from Liverpool Street. Completely free.

hidden_gems.name: Bunhill Fields
hidden_gems.description: A tiny historic burial ground where Daniel Defoe, William Blake, and John Bunyan are buried. Hidden in plain sight next to a busy junction.

markets.name: Maltby Street Market
markets.description: Best food market in London. Under railway arches, producer-direct, locals only vibe.

markets.name: Columbia Road Flower Market
markets.description: Victorian flower market, all independent traders. Come for the atmosphere as much as the flowers.

markets.name: Portobello Road Market
markets.description: Antiques, vintage clothing, food stalls. Saturday is peak — go early or go late to avoid the crush.

markets.name: Broadway Market
markets.description: East London's favourite Saturday market. Artisan food, coffee, vintage. Very local crowd.

markets.name: Brixton Market
markets.description: Caribbean and African food, independent fashion, record shops. The soul of south London.

seasonal_notes.label: Winter (Dec-Feb)
seasonal_notes.notes: Short days (dark by 4pm), cold and often wet. Push museums, galleries, and warm indoor venues. Winter markets in December. Avoid outdoor activities in mornings — start later. Christmas lights in Carnaby Street and Oxford Street are worth seeing in December.

seasonal_notes.label: Spring (Mar-May)
seasonal_notes.notes: Best time to visit London. Mild, parks blooming. Chelsea Flower Show in May. Push park walks, outdoor markets, Parkland Walk. Cherry blossom in Kyoto Garden, Holland Park in late March.

seasonal_notes.label: Summer (Jun-Aug)
seasonal_notes.notes: Long days (light until 9:30pm), warm but unpredictable. Outdoor cinema at Somerset House in July-August. Notting Hill Carnival in August Bank Holiday. Busiest tourist season — book restaurants further ahead. Evenings are magical for riverside walks.

seasonal_notes.label: Autumn (Sep-Nov)
seasonal_notes.notes: Underrated season. Crowds thin after September, parks turn golden. Affordable and atmospheric. Frieze Art Fair in October. Bonfire Night (5th November) is spectacular in Victoria Park or Primrose Hill.

local_transport_tips: Oyster card or contactless bank card — never buy paper tickets. Daily cap means unlimited travel after a threshold.
Night Tube runs Friday and Saturday nights on Central, Victoria, Jubilee, Northern, and Piccadilly lines.
Boris Bikes (Santander Cycles) are everywhere in Zone 1-2. First 30 minutes free with day pass.
Black cabs can be hailed anywhere. Uber works but local minicabs via the Bolt or Kapten app are cheaper.
The Elizabeth Line (Crossrail) is fast and modern — use it for east-west journeys.
Walking between most central attractions takes 15-25 minutes and is almost always faster than the tube for 1-2 stops.
